question: Why do you want to study Speech and Language Therapy?
lookingFor: A specific, genuine motivation grounded in real-world exposure — not a vague wish to help people.

bullets: Identify a concrete experience (volunteering, personal connection, observation) that sparked the interest
Articulate what draws you to SLT specifically over adjacent professions like teaching, occupational therapy, or nursing
Show you understand the breadth of the discipline — communication covers far more than speech
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: When my younger brother was diagnosed with a stammer at age seven, I watched his therapist do something I'd never seen before — she made him feel proud of his voice rather than ashamed of it, and the sessions changed how he moved through the world. That planted a seed I kept coming back to. I spent a summer volunteering at a community stroke centre and realised how central communication is to identity and independence — when someone can't express themselves, everything else is affected. I want to study SLT because it sits at that exact intersection of neuroscience, language, and human connection, and I haven't found another discipline that holds all three.

commonMistakes: 'I've always wanted to help people' with no field-specific reason
Reducing SLT to speech correction and ignoring language, eating, or swallowing
A motivation that sounds like it belongs on a nursing or teaching application
followUps: What aspect of SLT surprised you most when you started exploring the field?

question: Why Speech and Language Therapy rather than teaching, educational psychology, or occupational therapy?
lookingFor: A thoughtful comparison that shows the applicant has genuinely researched what SLT involves and can distinguish it from related roles.

bullets: Demonstrate knowledge of what each adjacent profession actually does
Name the specific aspect of SLT — assessment, dysphagia, AAC, the science of language — that isn't replicated elsewhere
Tie the distinction back to personal experience or a concrete reason
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I considered teaching and educational psychology seriously, and I can see why they might look similar from the outside. But what SLT offers that the others don't is the ability to work at the level of the communication itself — the underlying mechanism, not just the learning outcome. I'm fascinated by the clinical-science side: running an assessment, formulating a diagnosis, designing an intervention, and tracking whether it's working. That cycle of reasoning and adjustment is what I find most compelling. I also discovered that SLT caseloads include dysphagia and acquired neurological conditions, which aren't part of the other paths at all — and that breadth is exactly what keeps me wanting to learn more.

commonMistakes: Implying the other professions are less valuable
Comparing SLT to teaching without mentioning clinical and medical contexts
Not knowing that SLT covers dysphagia and AAC, not only speech
followUps: Did you shadow or research any of the other professions before settling on SLT?

question: Tell us about any experience you've had that's relevant to Speech and Language Therapy.
lookingFor: Specific, reflected-upon experience — the panel want to see what you learned, not just what you did.

bullets: Name the setting, the people, and what your role actually involved
Reflect on what you observed about communication and what it taught you
Connect the experience to an aspect of the course or profession
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I volunteered for two terms at a primary school's language-support unit, where I worked alongside the SENCO helping children with developmental language disorder access the mainstream curriculum. My job was mainly games and reading — but I started noticing patterns in how different children got stuck: some had word retrieval difficulties, some had difficulties with sentence structure, and some seemed to understand fine but couldn't formulate a response under time pressure. Those distinctions started to feel important, and when I read up on them I realised the SLT was the one mapping that out. The experience made me want to understand the science behind what I was observing, which is what pushed me to apply.

commonMistakes: Describing what you did without saying what you learned
Mentioning experience so briefly it seems like a checkbox rather than a genuine insight
Claiming SLT-level skills when your role was a support or assistant role
followUps: Was there a moment in that experience that genuinely surprised you?

question: What does a Speech and Language Therapist actually do on a typical working day?
lookingFor: An accurate picture of the breadth of SLT — clinical, collaborative, and administrative — that goes beyond the cliche of correcting lisps.

bullets: Cover at least two different clinical areas (e.g. paediatric language, acquired disorders, dysphagia, AAC)
Mention the collaborative dimension — working with teachers, nurses, carers, families
Acknowledge that SLTs work across very different settings (schools, hospitals, community clinics, care homes)
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: From what I've read and from speaking to an SLT, the day varies hugely by setting. A community SLT might spend the morning in a school doing language assessments with children who have DLD, and the afternoon in a care home working on safe swallowing with residents who have Parkinson's. A hospital SLT might be on an acute ward assessing someone post-stroke for communication and dysphagia, then attending a multidisciplinary team meeting to share the communication plan. There's a lot of report-writing and liaison — with teachers, consultants, families — alongside the face-to-face sessions. What I find exciting is that no two populations are the same, and the clinical reasoning shifts completely between contexts.

commonMistakes: Describing only children with articulation difficulties and missing the adult or dysphagia caseload
Underestimating the medical settings — SLTs work in hospitals and ICUs, not only schools
Not mentioning documentation, MDT working, or family-centred practice
followUps: Which setting do you most want to work in, and why?

question: What is Developmental Language Disorder, and why does it matter?
lookingFor: A working definition that goes beyond 'late talker', plus an appreciation of why DLD is significant and under-recognised.

bullets: Define DLD as a persistent difficulty with language that isn't explained by hearing loss, ASD, or other known conditions
Give a sense of prevalence — roughly 2 children in every 30 in a typical classroom
Explain the downstream impact: literacy, education, mental health, employment
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: DLD is a persistent, significant difficulty with language — spoken and/or written — that can't be explained by another condition like hearing impairment or intellectual disability. It's actually quite common — around two children in a typical UK class of 30 will have it — but it often goes unidentified because children can develop coping strategies that hide how much they're struggling. The impact is wide-reaching: difficulties following classroom instructions, reading comprehension, forming friendships through conversation, and accessing the curriculum in general. I came across the RALLI campaign, which has been pushing to raise awareness because DLD is still under-identified in schools, and that felt like exactly the kind of systemic problem where SLTs can make a difference.

commonMistakes: Confusing DLD with autism spectrum disorder or conflating the two
Treating it as simply a delay that children grow out of
Not knowing any figures for prevalence — suggesting the reading was very surface-level
followUps: How would you explain DLD to a worried parent in plain language?

question: Many people are surprised that SLTs work with swallowing difficulties. Why is dysphagia part of SLT, and what does that role involve?
lookingFor: An accurate explanation of the anatomical link between speech and swallowing, plus awareness of the clinical risks and SLT's role in managing them.

bullets: Explain that the oral, pharyngeal, and laryngeal structures serve both communication and swallowing
Name the main risk of dysphagia — aspiration and aspiration pneumonia
Describe what an SLT's dysphagia role actually involves: assessment, dietary texture modification, safe-swallowing strategies
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: It surprised me too at first, but once you look at the anatomy it makes complete sense. The throat, tongue, larynx, and soft palate are all used for both speech and swallowing — they're the same structures. Conditions like stroke, Parkinson's, head and neck cancer, or dementia can affect the muscles that coordinate the swallow, which means food or liquid can enter the airway rather than the oesophagus. That's aspiration, and it can lead to aspiration pneumonia, which is life-threatening. SLTs assess swallowing function — sometimes alongside instrumental assessment — and recommend dietary texture modifications, posture adjustments, or swallowing manoeuvres to keep people safe. It's one of the most medically acute parts of the job, and I find it compelling precisely because it shows how clinical SLT can be.

commonMistakes: Being genuinely unaware that SLTs work with swallowing — suggests very limited research into the profession
Knowing what dysphagia is but having no idea what the SLT actually does about it
Underplaying the clinical seriousness — aspiration pneumonia is a leading cause of death in stroke patients
followUps: How would you feel about working in an acute medical setting as part of this role?

question: What is Augmentative and Alternative Communication, and what is an SLT's role in it?
lookingFor: A clear explanation of AAC with examples, plus understanding that SLTs assess communication needs and support implementation — not just hand over a device.

bullets: Define AAC broadly — any method supporting communication beyond speech, from low-tech symbols to high-tech speech-generating devices
Give examples across the spectrum: signing, PECS, symbol boards, eye-gaze technology
Explain that the SLT's role includes assessment of communication needs, selection, programming, and ongoing support for the user and their network
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: AAC refers to any method or tool that supports or replaces speech for someone who can't communicate verbally, or whose speech isn't meeting their needs. It covers a huge range — from low-tech approaches like symbol boards and PECS, to sign systems like Makaton, to high-tech speech-generating devices controlled by eye-gaze or switches. The SLT's role goes beyond handing someone a device. They assess what kind of access and vocabulary the person needs, work with the family or support team to help everyone use it consistently, and adapt the system as the person's needs change. I find the AAC side of SLT genuinely exciting because it sits at the intersection of linguistics, technology, and inclusion — finding a voice for someone who didn't have one is a powerful thing.

commonMistakes: Thinking AAC is only for people with physical disabilities and missing the cognitive or language-based users
Describing AAC as 'just giving someone an iPad' without mentioning the clinical assessment and implementation work
Not knowing what PECS or Makaton are
followUps: Have you ever seen or used any AAC system, even informally?

question: How would you describe stammering to someone who knows nothing about it, and what does therapy for it typically involve?
lookingFor: A description that covers both the physical and psychosocial dimensions, with awareness that therapy isn't just about fluency.

bullets: Describe stammering as a neurological difference in speech fluency — not anxiety, not lack of confidence
Acknowledge the psychosocial impact: avoidance, identity, shame
Note that therapy goals are broader than fluency and might include acceptance, voluntary stammering, or managing avoidance
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Stammering is a disruption in the fluency of speech — it can involve repetitions, prolongations, or blocks where sound seems to get stuck. It's neurological in origin, not a sign of anxiety or low intelligence, though anxiety often develops as a result of how others react to it. What I find interesting is that SLT therapy for stammering isn't just about producing more fluent speech — for many people, especially older children and adults, the goal is broader: reducing avoidance, rebuilding confidence, and sometimes working toward accepting the stammer rather than eliminating it. Approaches like the Lidcombe Programme for young children, or Acceptance and Commitment Therapy frameworks for adults, show how different the aims can be at different life stages. The psychosocial dimension is as important as the technical one.

commonMistakes: Saying stammering is caused by nerves or a lack of confidence
Assuming the therapy goal is always fluency — many adults prefer acceptance-based approaches
Not knowing about the neurological basis of stammering
followUps: How would you feel if a client told you they wanted to stammer confidently rather than be fluent?

question: What is aphasia, and how does SLT support someone who has it after a stroke?
lookingFor: An accurate definition of aphasia (not to be confused with dysarthria or memory loss) plus a realistic picture of what SLT-supported recovery involves.

bullets: Define aphasia as an acquired language impairment affecting speaking, understanding, reading, and/or writing — not intelligence
Distinguish aphasia from dysarthria (motor speech) and from general cognitive decline
Describe SLT's role: assessment of language profile, communication strategies, conversation partner training, and the evidence base for neuroplasticity-based therapy
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Aphasia is an acquired impairment of language that typically follows stroke or brain injury. It can affect someone's ability to speak, understand others, read, or write — often in combination, and in different patterns depending on which part of the brain is damaged. It's important to understand that aphasia doesn't affect intelligence: the person is still entirely themselves, but the language system is disrupted. SLT support involves assessing the exact profile of the language impairment, then working on the specific areas — word-finding, sentence production, functional communication — using therapy approaches grounded in neuroplasticity. SLTs also train conversation partners — family members, carers — so they know how to support the person in everyday exchanges. I've read about constraint-induced aphasia therapy, which exploits neuroplasticity, and found the science behind recovery genuinely compelling.

commonMistakes: Confusing aphasia with dysarthria — they have different origins and different interventions
Implying the person has lost their intelligence or personality
Describing SLT support only in terms of exercises without mentioning conversation partner training or functional communication
followUps: How would you communicate with a patient who has very limited verbal output?

question: What is the SLT's role when working with autistic children or adults?
lookingFor: An understanding of pragmatic and social communication alongside a neurodiversity-affirming stance — the goal is not to 'normalise' autistic communication.

bullets: Identify pragmatic and social communication difficulties as the central area — not just vocabulary or grammar
Acknowledge that AAC and visual supports are often part of the toolkit
Reflect a neurodiversity-affirming approach: the goal is to support the autistic person's needs and communication, not to make them appear neurotypical
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: When working with autistic children or adults, SLTs tend to focus on pragmatic and social communication — how language is used in context, including turn-taking, understanding non-literal language, and using language to meet needs. The work might also involve AAC for those who are non-speaking, or visual supports that make communication more predictable and accessible. What I think is really important here is the neurodiversity-affirming lens: the SLT's job is to support the autistic person's ability to communicate in ways that work for them, not to train them to mask or to look neurotypical. I've read about the shift away from approaches like ABA that targeted eye contact, toward co-produced goals where the autistic person or their family has real input into what communication support looks like.

commonMistakes: Framing the SLT's goal as making autistic people communicate in neurotypical ways
Not mentioning pragmatic communication and focusing only on vocabulary or articulation
Being unaware that many autistic people are non-speaking and that AAC is central to the work
followUps: How would you feel if the goals a family wanted for their child conflicted with what you thought was best for the child?

question: What do you know about how children typically acquire language, and why does that knowledge matter to a speech and language therapist?
lookingFor: A grasp of the key milestones and underlying mechanisms — babbling, joint attention, fast-mapping — and an understanding of why typical development is the benchmark for clinical assessment.

bullets: Outline key milestones: babbling by 6 months, first words around 12 months, two-word combinations around 18–24 months, rapid vocabulary growth and grammar from 2–3 years
Mention at least one mechanism: joint attention, statistical learning, or fast-mapping
Explain why knowing the typical trajectory helps SLTs identify what is and isn't within range, and where specifically a child's development has diverged
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Typically developing children follow a broadly predictable trajectory: babbling and vocal play in the first six months, canonical babbling by around 7–8 months, first words around 12 months, and then a vocabulary spurt that accelerates from around 18 months onward. Two-word combinations usually appear around 18–24 months, and grammatical complexity grows quickly from there. One thing I find fascinating is how infants learn so rapidly — mechanisms like joint attention, where a baby follows a caregiver's gaze to map a word to an object, and fast-mapping, where a single exposure is enough to begin learning a word, must be doing a lot of work. For an SLT, having a detailed grasp of this typical trajectory is essential because it's the baseline for assessment — understanding exactly at what point and in what way a child's development has diverged is what shapes the therapy plan.

commonMistakes: Getting the milestones significantly wrong — for example, placing first words at 2 years
Describing acquisition as purely imitation and missing the generative, rule-governed nature of it
Not connecting the theoretical knowledge to the clinical application
followUps: Do you think language is innate, or is it entirely learned from the environment? What's the evidence?

question: How might being bilingual affect a child's communication development, and what challenges does this create for an SLT assessing them?
lookingFor: Understanding that bilingualism is not a disorder and is not a cause of language delay, alongside awareness of the assessment challenges it creates.

bullets: State clearly that bilingualism does not cause language disorder or delay
Explain that bilingual children may distribute their total vocabulary across two languages, so single-language assessments can underestimate their total competence
Note the challenge: standardised assessments are almost always normed on monolingual speakers, so referencing norms directly can lead to over-identification of disorder
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Bilingualism is a normal and common feature of language development — it doesn't cause language disorder. In fact, bilingual children's combined vocabulary across both languages is comparable to monolingual peers, though each individual language may have a smaller vocabulary than a monolingual child's. That immediately creates an assessment problem: most standardised SLT assessments are normed on monolingual speakers, so if you administer them in only one language you risk under-representing the child's true competence and falsely flagging a disorder. Good practice involves assessing in both languages where possible, gathering a detailed family history and language use profile, and paying attention to whether difficulties appear across both languages — which is more indicative of a true language disorder — or only in the weaker language, which might reflect exposure rather than impairment. It's an area where cultural competence and careful clinical reasoning are really important.

commonMistakes: Suggesting that raising children bilingually causes language delay or confusion — this is a myth
Not knowing about the norming problem with standardised assessments
Failing to mention that difficulties in both languages are more clinically significant than difficulties in only one
followUps: What would you do if no assessment tools existed in a child's home language?

question: What types of voice disorders do SLTs work with, and what does voice therapy typically involve?
lookingFor: An understanding that SLTs work with both functional and organic voice disorders, and that therapy involves more than just exercises.

bullets: Distinguish functional voice disorders (caused by vocal misuse or psychogenic factors) from organic ones (nodules, paralysis, cancer)
Mention that SLTs work alongside ENT surgeons and that instrumental assessment like laryngoscopy informs the therapy plan
Describe what voice therapy actually involves: vocal hygiene education, resonance, breath support, and reducing hyperfunctional patterns
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: SLTs work with a wide range of voice disorders. Functional disorders — where the voice mechanism is structurally intact but is being used inefficiently or under strain — include muscle tension dysphonia, which is very common in teachers and singers. Organic disorders involve structural changes like vocal nodules, vocal cord paralysis, or laryngeal cancer. The SLT typically works alongside an ENT consultant, using instrumental assessment like laryngoscopy to understand what's happening structurally before designing therapy. Voice therapy itself covers a lot: educating patients about vocal hygiene, addressing breath support and posture, and working on resonance and reducing hyperfunctional patterns. For post-surgical cases — like laryngectomy — the work can extend to alaryngeal voice, including tracheo-oesophageal voice and electrolarynx use. It's one of the more technical areas of SLT practice.

commonMistakes: Thinking SLTs only work with voice disorders in isolation from medical teams
Reducing voice therapy to vocal exercises without mentioning hygiene, breath support, or the psychosocial dimension
Not knowing that SLTs work with post-laryngectomy patients
followUps: How would you approach working with a patient who was psychologically distressed about a voice change?

question: What does evidence-based practice mean in SLT, and why is it important?
lookingFor: An understanding that EBP integrates research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values — not just 'following guidelines'.

bullets: Define EBP using its three components: best available research evidence, clinical expertise, and client preferences and values
Explain why EBP matters in SLT: resources are limited, some historical interventions lacked evidence, and clients deserve the most effective approaches
Show awareness of the challenges — SLT research bases vary in quality, and the evidence doesn't always match the clinical scenario in front of you
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Evidence-based practice in SLT means integrating three things: the best available research evidence, the clinician's own expertise and judgement, and the values and preferences of the client and their family. It's not just doing what the guidelines say — it requires active appraisal of research quality, critical thinking about whether a study's participants resemble your client, and real conversation with the client about what matters to them. It's important in SLT because resources are scarce and we need to know our time is spent on approaches that actually work. There have been interventions historically — some ABA approaches for autism, for example — that weren't grounded in good evidence and in hindsight caused harm. I find RCSLT's commitment to building the SLT evidence base genuinely exciting, because there are still clinical areas where the evidence is thin and that's real intellectual territory to work in.

commonMistakes: Defining EBP as 'following guidelines' without the client-values or clinical-judgement components
Not knowing that the evidence base in SLT is uneven — some areas are very well studied, others are not
Treating EBP as purely academic rather than something that shapes everyday clinical decisions
followUps: What would you do if a family asked you to use a therapy approach you knew had no evidence base?

question: Has anything you've read, watched, or listened to recently deepened your interest in Speech and Language Therapy?
lookingFor: A genuinely engaged reference to a specific source — book, podcast, paper, documentary — that shows the applicant is actively exploring the field.

bullets: Name a specific source — not just 'I read some articles online'
Explain what it argued or described and why it resonated
Connect it to a clinical or theoretical aspect of SLT the applicant wants to explore in their degree
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I've been listening to the RCSLT podcast, and there was an episode with a therapist working in a secure mental health setting where she described how communication difficulties are massively over-represented in forensic populations — something like 60 to 80 percent of people in the criminal justice system have communication needs that have never been identified. That blew me away. It made me realise how far SLT reaches beyond the clinic — into justice, into housing, into educational inequality. It connected to a book I read earlier in the year called 'Talking Voices' by Deborah Tannen, which explored how dialogue and misunderstanding shape our social world. Together they reinforced that communication is never just a technical problem — it's a social and ethical one, and that's what I want to study.

commonMistakes: Citing a source so vaguely it's clear they haven't actually read it
Choosing only pop-science or easily Googled facts rather than something that shows depth
Describing the source without connecting it to their motivation or to an aspect of the degree
followUps: Was there something in your reading that challenged an assumption you had about the field?

question: Do you think you'd prefer working with children or adults, and why?
lookingFor: Thoughtful reflection on both populations with honest self-awareness — not just a preference with no reasoning.

bullets: Reflect genuinely on the differences in the work across the two populations
Acknowledge the value of training in both areas even if one appeals more
Tie the preference to something real — an experience, a clinical interest, or the type of work that energises them
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Honestly I'm drawn to both for different reasons, which is partly why I'm glad the degree covers both. With children, what excites me is the early intervention dimension — reaching a child before their communication difficulty affects their literacy and self-confidence is powerful, and the rate of change with young children can be remarkable. With adults, especially acquired communication disorders post-stroke, what draws me is the complexity of working with someone who had full language before and now has to rebuild it — the resilience in that, and the depth of relationship that comes from working with someone long-term. If I had to choose today I'd say paediatric, mainly because of my experience in the school setting, but I'd feel incomplete as a therapist without training in both.

commonMistakes: Saying 'children' simply because 'I like working with kids' with no clinical reflection
Not knowing enough about adult SLT to reflect meaningfully on the difference
Being dismissive of one population in a way that would concern a panel
followUps: What qualities do you think are most important when working with adults with acquired communication difficulties?

question: SLTs work as part of multidisciplinary teams. What does that involve, and why is it important?
lookingFor: A realistic picture of MDT working in SLT — the roles involved, the communication required — plus an understanding of why collaboration matters for the client.

bullets: Name at least three professions SLTs commonly collaborate with — and vary them by setting
Explain the communication dimension: shared language, co-planning, negotiating roles
Give a reason why MDT working improves client outcomes, not just team morale
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: In a school setting an SLT might work alongside educational psychologists, SENCOs, and class teachers to make sure communication strategies are embedded in the classroom, not just in the therapy room. In a hospital setting they're part of teams with neurologists, occupational therapists, nurses, and dietitians — especially in stroke units or for dysphagia management where safe nutrition is a shared goal. What I think makes MDT working genuinely important for the client is that it prevents the 'silo' problem: if an SLT works out a communication approach in a clinic but the ward team doesn't know about it, the patient reverts in between sessions. When everyone is aligned on goals and strategies, the therapy generalises. It also means the SLT can offer something specific — the communication lens — without trying to do everything, which is a more effective use of expertise.

commonMistakes: Only naming one type of MDT context and missing the variety across settings
Describing MDT as just 'talking to other professionals' without explaining what the collaboration actually involves
Not connecting the collaboration to better outcomes for the client
followUps: What would you do if you disagreed with another team member's decision about a patient?

question: SLT can involve working with people in very difficult situations — bereavement, terminal illness, severe trauma. How would you manage the emotional demands of that?
lookingFor: Emotional maturity and self-awareness — acknowledging the difficulty without over-claiming resilience, and showing awareness of professional support structures.

bullets: Acknowledge that emotional weight is real and not something to be brushed off
Distinguish between empathy (feeling with someone) and over-identification that leads to burnout
Name concrete strategies: supervision, reflective practice, debrief with colleagues, self-care
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I think it would be dishonest to say it wouldn't affect me — some situations will be genuinely hard, and I don't think the goal is to be unaffected, it's to be able to manage it well. The distinction I keep coming back to is between empathy and over-identification. Empathy helps you do good work; losing yourself in a client's situation makes you less effective and risks burning out. From everything I've read and from speaking to practitioners, clinical supervision is a cornerstone of sustainable SLT practice — having a structured space to process difficult cases is the norm, not a sign of weakness. I've also built habits of looking after myself: I know that exercise and switching off between work and home are things I'll need to protect. I don't think I have all the answers yet, but I'm aware enough to know what the questions are.

commonMistakes: Claiming to be naturally resilient and not affected — this sounds unconvincing and unaware
Describing emotional management without mentioning supervision or professional structures
Conflating empathy with over-involvement as if they're the same thing
followUps: Tell me about a time when you found a situation emotionally hard and had to keep functioning.

question: Why do you want to study Speech and Language Therapy here specifically?
lookingFor: Evidence that the applicant has researched the specific course — modules, placements, staff research — beyond league tables.

bullets: Name a specific feature of the course — a module, a placement partnership, a research group, a clinical specialism represented by staff
Connect it to a personal interest or goal that the feature addresses
Show you know what makes this course different from alternatives you considered
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I was drawn initially by the placement diversity — the fact that the clinical hours span paediatric, adult neurological, and mental health settings means I'll graduate having tested my skills across genuinely different populations rather than in one narrow specialism. I also read about the research cluster on early language intervention, which directly overlaps with my interest in DLD and early identification in schools. What sealed it was reading the course handbook: the second-year module on multilingual communication aligns exactly with the kind of question I want to explore — how do we assess children fairly when our tools are built for monolingual English speakers. I can see myself genuinely engaging with the work here, not just completing it.

commonMistakes: Mentioning rankings or the city rather than the course itself
Praise that would fit any SLT programme in the country
Not knowing what distinguishes this course from alternatives — shows the choice wasn't deliberate
followUps: What would you contribute to the course community here?

question: An elderly client with mild cognitive impairment is assessed as being at risk of aspiration when eating, but refuses any modification to their diet. What do you do?
lookingFor: A reasoned approach to the tension between clinical safety and client autonomy — not a snap answer in either direction.

bullets: Acknowledge the genuine tension: the clinician's duty of care versus the client's right to make decisions about their own life
Note the importance of mental capacity assessment — not all cognitive impairment means the person lacks capacity
Describe a path: provide clear information, document the decision, involve the MDT and family, and respect a capacitous decision even if you disagree
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: This is a genuinely hard situation, and I think the first thing is to resist the urge to just override the refusal. The fact that someone has mild cognitive impairment doesn't automatically mean they lack capacity to make decisions about their diet — that's a separate, formal assessment, and it has to be specific to this decision. If they have capacity, they have the right to refuse, even if the risk is real. What I'd want to do is make sure the information has been communicated as clearly as possible — not just the clinical facts, but what aspiration pneumonia actually means in practice, for their quality of life. I'd document the conversation meticulously, involve the MDT and the family if appropriate, and perhaps explore whether there's a middle path — texture modifications they would accept, or compensatory strategies that reduce risk without removing the foods that matter to them. The goal is informed choice, not compliance.

commonMistakes: Immediately deferring to the client's refusal without exploring why or offering alternatives
Overriding the client's refusal because you disagree — this breaches autonomy without a capacity basis
Not mentioning mental capacity assessment or the legal framework around it
followUps: How would your approach change if the same client was assessed as lacking capacity?

question: What personal qualities do you think an effective SLT needs, and how do you demonstrate them?
lookingFor: Self-aware, specific qualities tied to real examples — not a list of generic attributes with no evidence.

bullets: Name three or four qualities and explain why each matters specifically in SLT
Back at least two of them with a concrete personal example
Avoid the generic list — 'patience, empathy, communication skills' — unless you can show rather than tell
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I think the quality that often gets underestimated is genuine curiosity about people. SLT draws on neuroscience and linguistics, but the job is ultimately about understanding a particular person in a particular context, and you can't do that without being genuinely interested in them. I've noticed that quality in myself when I'm volunteering — I'm always the one asking 'why' about what I observe. I'd also say adaptability: the caseload is so varied that rigid thinking doesn't serve you well, and I practised that during my school volunteer role when sessions I'd planned carefully fell apart the moment I met the child. And patience — real patience, not just tolerance — particularly for the kind of slow, non-linear progress that communication therapy often involves. I try to demonstrate that in everything from group work to how I handle being interrupted.

commonMistakes: Listing generic caring-profession qualities with nothing specific to SLT
Naming qualities without any evidence or example to support them
Being too modest — this is a chance to show yourself, not just describe an ideal therapist in the abstract
followUps: Which of those qualities do you think you still need to develop the most?

subtopic: Social model of disability and SLT
question: Some critics argue that SLT is too focused on fixing the individual rather than addressing the environments and systems that disable people with communication differences. How do you respond to that?
lookingFor: Critical engagement with the tension between medical and social models of disability — not a defensive defence of SLT, but a genuine grappling with the critique.

bullets: Show you know what the social model of disability actually says, and take it seriously
Acknowledge the genuine tension without dismissing either side
Describe how contemporary SLT practice tries to address both — through systemic advocacy, AAC access, and person-centred goal setting
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I think the critique has real force, and it's worth sitting with rather than deflecting. The social model of disability argues that it's not the impairment that disables people, it's the environment — inaccessible systems, attitudes, and structures. Applied to SLT, the argument is: why spend all your effort modifying the person's communication when the world could instead be designed to include different communicators? I don't think the answer is either/or. A child who can use AAC to express themselves is empowered by that regardless of whether the world is fully accessible. But the critique rightly challenges SLT to also work on the system — training teachers to include children with DLD, advocating for accessible communication in public services, designing environments where people with aphasia aren't excluded. The SLTs I find most compelling are the ones working at both levels: the individual and the structural. That dual lens is actually what draws me to the profession.

commonMistakes: Defending SLT without engaging with the substance of the social model argument
Accepting the critique completely and implying SLT has no value — equally unconvincing
Not knowing what the social model of disability is
followUps: Can you give an example of where an SLT might work to change an environment rather than the individual?

question: What is the difference between an articulation disorder and a phonological disorder, and why does that distinction matter clinically?
lookingFor: A clear conceptual distinction between a motor-level difficulty and a phonological system-level difficulty, with awareness of why the distinction shapes therapy.

bullets: Define articulation disorders as difficulties with the physical production of specific speech sounds — the motor execution level
Define phonological disorders as difficulties with the underlying rule system governing how sounds pattern in language — even when the child can physically produce the sounds
Explain that the distinction matters because the two require different therapy approaches
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: An articulation disorder is a difficulty at the motor level — a child physically cannot produce a specific sound accurately, perhaps because of how they're positioning their tongue or lips. An SLT working on articulation might use placement cues and structured practice to help the child learn the correct motor pattern. A phonological disorder is different: the child may be able to produce the sound in isolation, but their underlying phonological system has a rule that differs from the adult system — for example, consistently replacing all fricatives with stops, or omitting all final consonants. That's not a motor problem; it's a systematic patterning problem. The distinction matters clinically because the therapy is fundamentally different. Phonological therapy targets the underlying pattern — often using minimal pairs or metaphonological approaches — not the motor execution of individual sounds. Treating a phonological disorder as an articulation disorder would be treating the wrong level of the system.

commonMistakes: Conflating the two and treating all speech sound difficulties as the same
Knowing the definitions but not explaining why the distinction shapes therapy
Describing phonological disorder as simply a more severe articulation disorder
followUps: How would you assess whether a child's difficulty is at the articulation or phonological level?

question: What do you know about how the SLT profession is regulated in the UK, and why does regulation matter?
lookingFor: Knowledge of the HCPC as the statutory regulator and the RCSLT as the professional body — and a genuine understanding of why regulation protects clients.

bullets: Distinguish between the HCPC (statutory regulator — you must be registered to practice as an SLT) and the RCSLT (professional body — represents and supports members)
Explain what the HCPC registration requires: a recognised qualification, CPD, and fitness to practise
Articulate why regulation matters: public protection, professional standards, accountability
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: In the UK, SLTs are regulated by the Health and Care Professions Council — the HCPC. You can only use the protected title 'speech and language therapist' if you're registered with them, and registration requires a HCPC-approved degree, ongoing CPD, and meeting their standards of conduct and fitness to practise. The RCSLT — the Royal College of Speech and Language Therapists — is the separate professional body that advocates for the profession, sets clinical guidelines, and supports members. Regulation matters because it protects the public: if an SLT causes harm or acts outside their competence, the HCPC can investigate and remove them from the register. It also raises the overall standard of practice — knowing you're accountable to a regulatory body encourages ongoing learning and careful record-keeping. I think that accountability structure is something I find reassuring as a future practitioner, not a burden.

commonMistakes: Confusing the HCPC and RCSLT or not knowing both exist
Thinking SLT is self-regulated with no statutory body
Knowing the acronyms without understanding what they actually do
followUps: What does continuing professional development look like for an SLT once they're qualified?

question: What do you see as one of the biggest challenges currently facing the Speech and Language Therapy profession?
lookingFor: Genuine field awareness — a specific, informed challenge with understanding of why it exists and what it means for practice.

bullets: Name a specific challenge — workforce shortages, NHS waiting lists, funding for community SLT, the post-pandemic communication backlog in children
Show understanding of the structural reason behind it
Reflect on what it means for practice or for the kind of SLT they want to become
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: One thing that stands out to me is the post-pandemic impact on children's communication development. The combination of reduced social interaction, disrupted schooling, and the effect of masks on face-to-face communication meant that many children arrived in reception with significantly below-expected communication skills. SLT services that were already stretched before 2020 are now facing a wave of referrals with no corresponding increase in workforce. I read RCSLT's reports on waiting times in community paediatric SLT, and in some areas families wait over a year for an initial assessment. What that means practically is that children's language difficulties compound during that wait — literacy development is affected, and by the time they're seen the gap is wider. It raises a question I think about a lot: how do SLTs prioritise, and what role can we play in upskilling teachers and parents to provide more support in the interim? It's a systemic challenge as much as a clinical one.

commonMistakes: Naming a vague 'funding pressures' without any specific knowledge of what this means for SLT
Being unaware of the post-pandemic language development crisis in children, which has been widely reported
Identifying the challenge but having nothing to say about what it means for how SLTs might respond
followUps: If you were advising a government minister on SLT workforce policy, what would be your first recommendation?
